input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The **System Under Study (SUS)** is a **lab-scale gantry crane**, a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) designed to simulate container handling operations inspired by harbor cranes in the Port of Antwerp. It consists of a **physical gantry crane** (including its controller) and a **digital twin (DT)** that mirrors its behavior. The physical system is a scaled-down version of industrial cranes, featuring a **Crane Frame**, **two stepper motors** (one for cart movement and one for hoisting), **motor controllers (TMC4671-Eval)**, an **electromagnet** for container pickup, a **swing angle encoder** to measure hoist swinging motion, and an **anemometer** to measure wind speed. The DT is implemented as a modular system with services for optimal control, historical data logging, visualization, and continuous validation, enabling bidirectional interaction between the physical and digital domains.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The **physical acting components** in the DT constellation include:
  - **Two stepper motors** (cart motor and hoist motor) controlled via **TMC4671-Eval motor controllers** for precise movement.
  - An **electromagnet** at the hoist end for picking up and releasing containers (pucks).
  - An **Arduino-based control system** interfacing with the motors, electromagnet, and sensors to execute commands from the **Control Application** in the DT.
  - A **Power Supply Unit (PSU)** to power the electronics. These components enable the DT to **act on the physical system** via trajectory commands, control signals, and real-time adjustments.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The **physical sensing components** include:
  - A **swing angle encoder** to measure the swinging motion of the hoist, critical for validating trajectory accuracy and detecting faults.
  - An **anemometer** to measure wind speed, which affects crane dynamics and is logged for simulation validation.
  - **Motor controllers (TMC4671-Eval)** with feedback mechanisms to monitor motor performance and position.
  - An **Arduino-based measurement system** that collects sensor data (swing angle, wind speed) and transmits it to the **Logging Application** in the DT via the **Physical Crane Interface**.
  These sensors provide real-time data for **physical-to-virtual interaction**, enabling the DT to validate simulations and detect anomalies.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** is facilitated through:
  - **Continuous data streaming** from the **Physical Crane Interface** (a Python program) to the **Logging Application**, which logs crane state (position, speed, swing angle, wind speed) into a **TimescaleDB database** at high frequency.
  - **MQTT-based signaling** between the **Physical Crane Interface** and other DT services (e.g., **Continuous Validation Service**, **Trajectory Generator Service**) for real-time synchronization.
  - **Event-driven updates** triggered by sensor readings (e.g., swing angle deviations, wind speed changes) to ensure the DT reflects the physical system’s state dynamically.
  The interaction is bidirectional in design, with the **Control Application** sending commands back to the physical system while receiving feedback for validation.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** is implemented via:
  - **Optimal trajectory commands** generated by the **Trajectory Generator Service**, which uses a **kinematic model** of the crane to compute swing-free paths. These trajectories are sent to the **Control Application**, which translates them into motor control signals.
  - **Real-time adjustments** from the **Continuous Validation Service**, which compares simulated trajectories (from the **Simulation Service**) with executed trajectories (from sensor data) and triggers alerts if discrepancies exceed thresholds.
  - **Human Machine Interface (HMI) commands** (e.g., manual overrides, homing/zeroing) sent to the **Control Application**, which directly influences the physical crane’s actuators.
  The interaction relies on **Python-based control loops** and **MQTT messaging** for low-latency communication.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The DT provides the following **services**:
  1. **Optimal Trajectory Generation**: Uses a **kinematic model** to compute swing-free movement paths for the crane, minimizing residual oscillations.
  2. **Historical Data Logging**: Stores crane state data (position, speed, swing angle, wind speed) in a **TimescaleDB database** for post-analysis.
  3. **Data Visualization**: A **Grafana dashboard** displays real-time and historical trajectories, with simulated confidence bounds for validation.
  4. **Continuous Validation**: Compares executed trajectories (from sensors) with simulated trajectories (from the **Simulation Service**) using distance metrics. Alerts are triggered via the dashboard if deviations exceed thresholds.
  5. **Simulation Service**: Models crane dynamics (including wind effects) to predict behavior under different conditions.
  6. **Fault Detection**: Extends continuous validation with **time-series classification techniques** to localize faults (e.g., sensor failures, motor anomalies).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale:
The **twinning time scale** is **real-time with millisecond-to-second granularity**:
  - **Data logging** occurs at high frequency (e.g., sensor readings every 10–100ms) to capture dynamic crane behavior.
  - **Trajectory execution and validation** are synchronized with the physical system’s operation cycle (typically <1s for movement commands).
  - **Continuous validation** runs in near-real-time, comparing simulated and executed trajectories with sub-second latency.
  - **MQTT messaging** ensures low-latency communication between services, while the **TimescaleDB database** is optimized for time-series queries.
  The system is designed for **online synchronization**, where the DT’s services adapt to the physical system’s state in real-time.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities:
The DT is implemented with **centralized multiplicities** but modular components:
  - **Six DT containers** (Database, Dashboard, Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation, MQTT Broker) are deployed as **independent services** but interact via a shared **MQTT broker** and **TimescaleDB database**.
  - The **Physical System** (5 containers: Physical Crane, Crane Interface, Control App, Logging App, HMI) operates independently but synchronizes with the DT via the **Physical Crane Interface**.
  - **No decentralized twins** are explicitly mentioned; instead, the system uses a **monolithic yet modular architecture** where services can be extended or replaced without full redesign.
  - The **gantrylib Python module** encapsulates core DT logic, allowing for **horizontal scaling** of services (e.g., adding more validation nodes).
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The DT covers the following **life-cycle stages** of the physical system:
  1. **As Designed**: Represented by **CAD models (Autodesk Fusion360)** and **kinematic models** used for trajectory planning and simulation.
  2. **As Manufactured**: Validated via **continuous validation** during initial deployment, ensuring the physical crane matches the digital model.
  3. **As Operated**: Continuously monitored through **real-time data logging**, **fault detection**, and **adaptive control** (e.g., adjusting trajectories based on wind conditions).
  The DT does **not** explicitly model **as-maintained** stages (e.g., wear-and-tear), but its **continuous validation** and **fault detection** services implicitly support lifecycle management by identifying deviations from expected behavior.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The DT includes the following **models and data**:
  - **CAD Models**: 3D representations of the crane (stored in **Autodesk Fusion360**) for visualization and simulation.
  - **Kinematic Model**: A mathematical model of the crane’s movement dynamics, used by the **Trajectory Generator Service** to compute optimal paths.
  - **Simulation Model**: A physics-based model (including wind effects) implemented in the **Simulation Service** to predict crane behavior.
  - **Time-Series Data**: Logged in **TimescaleDB** (a PostgreSQL extension) with high-frequency sensor readings (swing angle, wind speed, motor positions).
  - **Arcadia Models**: **Capella-based MBSE models** (stored in the **arcadia** folder) for systems engineering analysis, including behavioral and structural representations.
  - **Historical Trajectories**: Stored for post-mortem analysis and validation of the **Continuous Validation Service**.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **tooling and enablers** include:
  - **Programming**: Python (for all DT services, control logic, and logging) bundled in the **gantrylib** module.
  - **Containerization**: **Docker** for deploying the **Database (TimescaleDB)**, **MQTT Broker (Eclipse Mosquitto)**, and **Dashboard (Grafana)** via a **docker-compose** file.
  - **Database**: **TimescaleDB** (PostgreSQL-based) for time-series data storage, chosen for SQL compatibility and time-series optimization.
  - **Visualization**: **Grafana** for real-time and historical data dashboards, with support for multiple data sources.
  - **Messaging**: **MQTT (Eclipse Mosquitto)** for lightweight, publish-subscribe communication between services.
  - **Hardware**: **Arduino** for sensor interfacing and control, with custom **KiCad-designed shields** for connectivity.
  - **Modeling**: **Autodesk Fusion360** for CAD, **Capella** (via Arcadia) for MBSE, and **C4 Model** for architecture documentation.
  - **Version Control**: **GitHub** for open-source collaboration, with **MIT License** for reproducibility.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The **DT constellation** is organized as a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** with the following architecture:
  - **Physical Layer**: The **gantry crane** (hardware) and its **Arduino-based controller**.
  - **Interface Layer**: The **Physical Crane Interface** (Python) bridges the physical and digital systems.
  - **Digital Twin Layer**: Comprising **six services** (Database, Dashboard, Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation, MQTT Broker) deployed on a **single personal computer** (or containerized for scalability).
  - **Human Interaction Layer**: The **Human Machine Interface (HMI)** for operators and researchers.
  The constellation follows a **service-oriented architecture**, where each component is loosely coupled via **MQTT** and **shared database**. The **C4 Model** diagrams (context, container, component) formalize this structure.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The **twinning process** involves:
  1. **Development**: Iterative refinement of the **kinematic model**, **simulation service**, and **validation logic**, as documented in publications [4]–[7].
  2. **Quality Assurance**: **Continuous validation** compares executed vs. simulated trajectories, with thresholds for fault detection.
  3. **Evolution**: The **DarTwin notation** was used to formalize **digital twin evolution patterns**, including:
     - **Model refinement** (e.g., adding wind effects to the simulation).
     - **Service extension** (e.g., integrating fault classification).
     - **Architecture scaling** (e.g., containerizing services for future deployment).
  The process is **open-source and reproducible**, with milestones documented in academic publications and GitHub commits. Future work includes **physical simulation emulation** and **custom hardware development** to reduce costs.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The **fidelity and validity** of the DT are ensured through:
  - **Model Validation**: The **Continuous Validation Service** compares executed trajectories (from sensors) with simulated trajectories (from the **Simulation Service**) using **distance metrics** (e.g., Euclidean distance between paths). Thresholds trigger alerts if deviations exceed acceptable limits.
  - **Uncertainty Quantification**: The **Simulation Service** includes **confidence bounds** (visualized in Grafana) to represent model uncertainty due to factors like wind speed.
  - **Verification**: The **Trajectory Generator Service** uses a **kinematic model** validated against real-world crane behavior, ensuring trajectories are physically feasible.
  - **Fault Detection**: **Time-series classification techniques** (e.g., from [6]) identify anomalies in sensor data, improving diagnostic fidelity.
  - **Reproducibility**: The exemplar is **open-source**, with all models (CAD, kinematic, simulation) and data (TimescaleDB logs) publicly available for validation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **technical connection** between the physical and digital systems is established via:
  - **Physical Crane Interface**: A Python program that **polling-based or event-driven** reads sensor data (swing angle, wind speed) from the **Arduino** and sends control commands to the **motor controllers (TMC4671-Eval)**.
  - **MQTT Protocol**: Used for **lightweight, low-latency messaging** between services (e.g., **Trajectory Generator → Control App**, **Logging App → Database**).
  - **TimescaleDB**: Acts as a **centralized data store** for time-series synchronization, with SQL queries for cross-service data access.
  - **Network Architecture**: Services run on a **single personal computer** (or containerized), with **local network communication** (no cloud dependency). The **docker-compose** setup ensures isolated but interconnected service deployment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **DT hosting and deployment** follows a **local, containerized approach**:
  - **Physical System**: Deployed as standalone hardware (crane, Arduino, motor controllers) connected to a **personal computer**.
  - **Digital Twin Services**: Hosted on the same computer via:
    - **Docker Containers**: **TimescaleDB**, **Eclipse Mosquitto (MQTT Broker)**, and **Grafana Dashboard** are containerized using a **docker-compose.yml** file for one-command deployment.
    - **Python Module (gantrylib)**: Core DT logic (control, logging, validation) is installed via **pip** and runs as local processes.
  - **Scalability**: The architecture supports **horizontal scaling** (e.g., adding more validation nodes) by extending the **MQTT-based service mesh**.
  - **No Cloud Dependency**: The system is designed for **local deployment**, though future work may explore cloud integration for distributed scenarios.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The **insights and decision-making** outputs of the DT include:
  - **Trajectory Optimization**: The **Trajectory Generator Service** provides **swing-free paths** to minimize energy consumption and operational time.
  - **Fault Localization**: The **Continuous Validation Service** and **time-series classification** identify **specific faults** (e.g., sensor drift, motor jamming) and their root causes.
  - **Operational Alerts**: The **Grafana Dashboard** visualizes **deviations from expected behavior**, enabling operators to take corrective actions (e.g., recalibrating sensors).
  - **Historical Analysis**: Logged data in **TimescaleDB** supports **post-mortem investigations** (e.g., analyzing crane performance under varying wind conditions).
  - **Model Improvement**: Insights from **continuous validation** feed back into **kinematic and simulation models** for iterative refinement.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The **horizontal integration** with external systems is limited but extensible:
  - **No explicit integration** with other DTs or enterprise systems is documented in the exemplar.
  - **Open APIs**: The **gantrylib Python module** and **MQTT broker** could theoretically integrate with external systems (e.g., **ERP for logistics**, **IoT platforms**).
  - **Data Export**: TimescaleDB supports **SQL queries**, allowing external tools to access logged crane data.
  - **Future Extensions**: The exemplar’s **harbor system expansion** (for teaching) suggests potential integration with **ship DTs** or **container tracking systems** via standardized interfaces (e.g., **OPC UA**, **AutomationML**).
  - **Current State**: The system is **self-contained**, with integration left as a future work item.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy:
The **data ownership and privacy** aspects are as follows:
  - **Open-Source License**: The exemplar is released under the **MIT License**, allowing unrestricted use, modification, and distribution.
  - **Data Ownership**: The **physical system owner** (e.g., researcher or educator) retains ownership of the data generated by the crane (e.g., sensor logs, trajectories).
  - **Privacy Considerations**: No **personally identifiable information (PII)** is collected. The system is designed for **lab-scale use**, with no sensitive data exposure.
  - **Access Control**: The **Grafana Dashboard** and **MQTT Broker** are deployed locally, with no public cloud exposure. Authentication is handled via **local user credentials** (not documented in detail).
  - **Compliance**: No explicit compliance with **GDPR** or other regulations is mentioned, as the use case is **non-commercial and academic**.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The **standardization** efforts include:
  - **Modeling**: Uses **AutomationML** (implied via related work [14]) and **Capella (Arcadia)** for MBSE, though the exemplar itself does not enforce strict standards.
  - **Architecture**: Follows the **C4 Model** for documentation, which is a **de facto standard** for software architecture but not a formal DT standard.
  - **Data Formats**: Relies on **open-source tools** (TimescaleDB, MQTT, Grafana) with no proprietary formats.
  - **Interoperability**: The **gantrylib module** and **MQTT broker** could integrate with **standardized DT frameworks** (e.g., **Eclipse Ditto**, **BaSyx**) in future extensions.
  - **Current State**: The exemplar is **ad-hoc** rather than standardized, prioritizing **reproducibility** over compliance with industry DT frameworks.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The **security and safety considerations** are as follows:
  - **Safety**:
    - The **physical crane** is designed for **lab-scale use** with **low operational speeds** to minimize risks.
    - **Emergency stops** are implied via the **HMI** (though not explicitly detailed).
    - **Fault detection** (via time-series classification) helps prevent unsafe operations (e.g., by alerting on motor failures).
  - **Security**:
    - **Local Deployment**: The system runs on a **personal computer** with no internet exposure, reducing cyberattack surfaces.
    - **MQTT Broker**: Uses **Eclipse Mosquitto** with default security settings (no TLS/SSL documented).
    - **Database**: **TimescaleDB** is password-protected but lacks advanced security features (e.g., encryption at rest).
    - **Remote Operations**: The DT can **remotely adjust crane parameters** (e.g., via HMI), but **no autonomous remote control** is implemented. Human oversight is required for critical actions.
    - **Future Work**: No explicit plans for **cybersecurity hardening** (e.g., authentication, audit logs) are mentioned, as the exemplar is **research-focused** rather than production-ready.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar Joost Mertens _Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_ _University of Antwerp_ Antwerpen, Belgium joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be _**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a** **large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly** **available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and** **reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-** **scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises** **both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical** **side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital** **side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,** **and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,** **data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup** **as use case in several previous publications where its functionality** **was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other** **freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope** **it can be used for future research or education on the topic of** **digital twins.** _**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-** **physical-system** I. I NTRODUCTION The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual systems and services derived therefrom. During operation data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3]. Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating research results requires relevant use cases. Such research results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the following digital twin related research: continuous validation methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a notation for system evolution [7]. In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in various domains would help reduce development overhead, and additionally also also help support the generalizability of research results. Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education is another field for which well described exemplars can be Joachim Denil _Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_ _University of Antwerp_ Antwerpen, Belgium joachim.denil@uantwerpen.be Fig. 1. The physical gantry crane. beneficial, aiding teachers with explaining and demonstrating the digital twin concept, and training engineers with the necessary experience to set up such systems in the real world. In this paper, we present an openly available digital twin exemplar of a lab-scale gantry crane. It is inspired by cranes in the harbor of Antwerp, which move containers between moored ships and the quay. It is a typical Cyber-Physical System and consists of: _•_ The physical gantry crane including its controller. _•_ The crane’s digital twin containing CAD models, services for data logging, data visualization, optimal trajectory generation, and continuous validation. The exemplar is hosted on Cosys-Lab’s organizational GitHub [1] . A picture of the physical crane is shown in fig. 1. The remainder of this paper is structured as follows: section II first discusses related exemplars from literature. Section III discusses our exemplar in detail, both architecturally and implementation wise. Afterwards section IV discusses the results and the publications that feature this exemplar. Next, section V discusses some limitations and future extensions. Finally section VI concludes the paper. 1 [https://github.com/Cosys-Lab/lab-scale-gantry-crane](https://github.com/Cosys-Lab/lab-scale-gantry-crane) II. B ACKGROUND & R ELATED W ORK A search with the query “digital twin exemplar” on Semantic Scholar, Google Scholar and GitHub’s search function yielded other openly available digital twin exemplars with accompanying publications. In what follows, we briefly discuss the most noteworthy ones. _A. ARCHES PiCar-X_ The PiCar-X is a little Raspberry-Pi based robotic car that is used by Barbie and Hasselbring as a demonstrator of various digital twin concepts [8], [9]. These digital twin concepts [10] were originally used in the ARCHES project on autonomous robotic networks [11], but the authors wanted to make these results reproducible in a lab-scale setting with an affordable and straightforward example. The exemplar is hosted on GitHub [2] and contains documentation on how to get the exemplar up and running. Emulators are also provided such that the physical system is not necessarily needed. The exemplar features technologies such as Docker containerization to encapsulate the examples and is built on the open ARCHES Digital Twin Framework which is built on top of the ROS (Robot Operation System) middleware. _B. GreenhouseDT_ GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse [12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with prerecorded data. _C. Air Quality Management_ The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14]. Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented digital twin pipeline. _D. Discussion_ The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses 2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X) 3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT) 4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar) Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system. a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are more ad-hoc. In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework. The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars. III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves small pucks which represents containers from one location to another. In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and describe the architecture of the system with a context and container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system. _A. Architecture_ Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital twin research on it. Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to keep the diagram readable. The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital twin if needed, consists of five containers. **Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane. 5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/) 6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for something that must be there for the system to work. Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system. **Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the crane. **Control Application:** a Python application that uses the crane interface to control the crane. **Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database. **Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the operator to configure and control the crane. The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe them bottom up. **Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data created by the system and its various services. **Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from the database. **Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no residual swinging motion at the end. **Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator **Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics between them and compares the value with an acceptable threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action. **MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between services. Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give an overview of the components powering it. We should note that for this physical system, we take a liberal interpretation of what goes in a C4 Model component diagram. Figure 4 shows this component diagram, again for brevity and readability only the most necessary relationships are shown. It contains the **Crane Frame** consisting of all the hardware constituting the physical structure of the crane. Two **Motors** and associated **Controllers**, one to control the cart’s movement, one to control the hoisting movement. An **Electromagnet** at the end of the hoist to pick up containers, a **Swing Angle Encoder** measures the swinging motion of the hoist, and an **Anemometer** measures the wind speed. An **Arduino** measures and controls those last three components. Lastly a **PSU** powers the electronics of the crane. _B. Implementation_ Regarding the implementation, we wanted to keep the operation of the system as simple as possible, that is, plug in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of the software on that computer. Figure 5 shows the distribution of the C4 Containers visually. For the implementation, we bundled all the Python C4 Containers in a Python module named _gantrylib_ . To use this module, it suffices to install it with _pip_ . This way it remains easy for researchers to implement additional digital twin services. For ease of deployment, the **Database**, **MQTT Broker** and **Dashboard** C4 Containers are containerized with Docker. A docker compose file is provided to bring the containers up and down with a single command. For the first time setup, all of the database and dashboard initialization are also automatically handled by this docker compose file. Furthermore some of the components warrant a small explanation for their implementation choices. For the database, the choice for TimescaleDB was driven by three factors: (i) it’s based on PostgreSQL, allowing for commonly known SQL queries for access. (ii) it’s optimized for time series data, which is what is logged. (iii) it’s open source. For the dashboard, the choice for Grafana was made because of (i) it’s wide support for data sources and (ii) again the fact that it’s open source. Lastly, for the MQTT Broker, the ubiquitous and open-source Eclipse Mosquitto was used. _C. Repository Organization_ Listing 1 shows the repository organization. The **BOM** folder contains the Bill-of-Materials. The **C4-diagrams** folder contains the diagrams used in this paper. The **CAD** folder contains CAD models of the crane made in Autodesk Fu sion360. The **arcadia** folder contains Arcadia models made in Capella for a lecture on Model-Based Systems Engineering that starred the crane. The **arduino** folder contains the code to deploy on the measurement and control Arduino, as well as hardware schematics and a board for a custom shield made in KiCad that simplifies connectivity of the sensor to the Arduino. The **docker** folder contains the docker compose file and all associated first-time setup files for the dashboard, database and MQTT broker. The **docs** folder contains the documentation made in MkDocs and hosted on GitHub Pages. The **examples** folder contains basic examples of the gantrylib module. The **gantrylib** folder then contains the module itself. The **test** folder contains test associated with the gantrylib module. The **LICENSE** file contains the MIT license under which the exemplar is published. The **README** gives a brief overview of the repository and redirects to github pages for the installation. Lastly, **mkdocs.yml** contains some MkDocs configuration and **setup.py** contains the metadata of the gantrylib module. **Listing 1** Repository organization. ./ |-- BOM/ |-- C4-diagrams |-- CAD/ |-- arcadia/ |-- arduino/ |-- docker/ |-- docs/ |-- examples/ |-- gantrylib/ |-- tests/ |-- LICENSE |-- README.md |-- mkdocs.yml |-- setup.py IV. R ESULTS Figures 6 to 8 show some results or output of this exemplar. Figure 6 shows the basic but functional Human Machine |Cart Motor Controller

The Human Machine Interface. Fig. 7. Docker compose startup. Interface the operator can use to control the crane. In it are controls for the crane’s movement, homing and zeroing, the real-time status and configurations of the logger w.r.t database writeout. Figure 7 shows that with the single docker compose command the containers and the associated network are up and running. Lastly, fig. 8 shows some traces on the dashboard. Those traces have also been simulated by the simulator, as indicated by the upper (in blue) and lower (in yellow) confidence bounds derived from the simulation. Fig. 8. Grafana Dashboard showing trajectory traces. _A. Usage in Publications_ The first publication using the exemplar is [4], a short abstract on the concept of continuous validation. While the paper itself did not yet feature the gantry crane, the accompanying poster used the crane as illustrative example. Later, in [5], we further explored the concept of continuous validation through the reuse of existing model validation techniques. Here the initial version of the crane exemplar was developed and used in the evaluation of the paper. For more detailled results w.r.t. the continuous validation digital twin we refer the reader to this publication. The next use of the crane was in [7], where we explored DarTwin, a notation for digital twin evolution. We identified multiple commonly used digital twin evolution. On the one hand the development process of the crane was used to derive those evolutions, on the other hand we envisioned multiple future evolutions of the crane system to demonstrate the DarTwin notation. The latest use of the exemplar was in [6], where we explored state-of-the-art time series classification techniques to find faults in systems in operation. In essence, we extended the continuous validation method of [5] with classification techniques to better pinpoint system faults in the exemplar. _B. Usage in Teaching_ At the University of Antwerp, we organized a Blended Intensive Programme (BIP) on the topic of Model Based Systems Engineering for Digital Twin Systems. Here the exemplar was used as project to work on. We expanded it to an entire harbor system, with a container identification system to intake containers in the harbor, and a ship digital twin with a buoyancy model to teach about correct container loading order. This BIP was organized during the first semester of academic year 2024-2025. In academic year 2025-2026 it will be revisited in the second semester. At the Gran Sasso Science Institute (GSSI) the exemplar was used during the SpaceRaise doctoral school to teach about digital twinning in practice [7] . On this first day talks were given on the topic of twinning in Systems Engineering, and the exemplar was used to practically summarize the topics presented that day. Specifically for this doctoral school, the Capella models were developed. V. D ISCUSSION AND F UTURE W ORK The gantry crane has proven it’s merit for both research and education, but nonetheless it has some limitations or shortcomings which we discuss here. _A. Cost_ Firstly, with the current BOM the crane is not low cost, at approximately 1250 Euro for one crane in parts. Almost half of this cost stems from the Trinamic Motor Drivers, for which a development kit is used. The logical next step is to forego the development kits and the measurement Arduino 7 [https://spaceraise.academy/#courses Week 2, May 19th 2025](https://spaceraise.academy/#courses) with its shield, and develop a custom driver board integrating these three microcontrollers. This would greatly bring the cost down. _B. Size_ The current size of the crane was chosen based on the dimensions of the location in the lab. This size, however, is larger than the common size of checked luggage accepted by airlines, making it cumbersome to transport. _C. Teaching Extensions_ During teaching we made extensions to the gantry crane: for the BIP more of the harbor was included, and for the doctoral school we focused on Capella models. We deemed those extensions a little too immature to include as features in this paper, in the future they must be further developed, especially the ship and harbor simulations for the BIP. _D. Lack of Physical Simulation_ Currently, there is no physical crane simulator. To make the exemplar even more accessible, a completely simulated version of the physical crane could be included, as was done in the exemplars mentioned in the related work. This researchers or educators can test the project without committing to building the rather costly crane. This could also open up uses on the topic of virtual commissioning. _E. Digital Twin Frameworks_ Over the past years a multitude of digital twin frameworks have been developed, many of them open-source [15], [16]. Examples are Eclipse Ditto, for Internet-of-Things devices and their digital twins, or Eclipse BaSyx for Asset Administration Shell driven digital twins. Our approach is much more ad-hoc, and more in line with the approach taken in [17]. We make use of open-source technologies, but none of them are tailored specifically to digital twins. One could argue for or against this, in our opinion the ad-hoc approach allows more freedom and less overhead, but usage of the frameworks would surely make the exemplar more industry relevant. VI. C ONCLUSION We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed to support digital twin research and education. It expands the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case, reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility was illustrated with examples from our personal academic publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar, it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which we provided pointers for future work.
